articles.headline: Mojtaba Khamenei Named Iran's Supreme Leader After Father's Death
articles.subheadline: The 56-year-old cleric, long described as 'the power behind the robes,' succeeds Ali Khamenei amid war and internal crisis
articles.body:
Mojtaba Khamenei, the reclusive 56-year-old son of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, has been selected as Iran's new supreme leader following his father's death in US-Israeli military strikes last week — a succession that breaks with the Islamic Republic's founding principle that its highest office should never become hereditary.

The appointment was made by the Assembly of Experts, the clerical body constitutionally empowered to choose Iran's supreme leader. Mojtaba Khamenei has never held government office, never given a public speech or interview, and has been seen in only a handful of published photographs and videos over several decades. Yet leaked US diplomatic cables published by WikiLeaks described him as a "capable and forceful" figure who wielded enormous influence within the regime — earning the moniker "the power behind the robes."

Born in 1969 in the northeastern city of Mashhad, Mojtaba is the second of Ali Khamenei's six children. He served briefly in the military during the Iran-Iraq War as a teenager and did not begin formal seminary studies in the holy city of Qom until he was 30 — an unusually late start. He remains a mid-ranking cleric, a status that could undermine his legitimacy in a system that demands high religious authority from its leader. In the weeks before his appointment, some Iranian media outlets and officials close to power centers began referring to him as "Ayatollah," a senior title he had not previously held — a move observers interpreted as a deliberate effort to bolster his credentials. His father underwent a similar rapid elevation upon becoming supreme leader in 1989.

Mojtaba's political influence has long been a subject of controversy. During the 2005 presidential election, reformist candidate Mehdi Karroubi publicly accused him of manipulating the vote through the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and the Basij militia to secure Mahmoud Ahmadinejad's victory. The same accusations resurfaced during the disputed 2009 election, which triggered the mass Green Movement protests. Former deputy interior minister Mostafa Tajzadeh, who was imprisoned for seven years, attributed his detention to "the direct wish of Mojtaba Khamenei."

The new supreme leader inherits a nation in profound crisis. Iran is enduring sustained US-Israeli military strikes — including overnight attacks on fuel depots in Tehran and Karaj — alongside deep economic distress and widespread public disillusionment with the regime. Reports indicate that Mojtaba lost not only his father but also his mother and wife in the strikes, a personal toll that analysts say makes any rapprochement with Western powers unlikely. Israel's defense minister warned last week that whoever succeeded Ali Khamenei would be "an unequivocal target for elimination," placing the untested leader under immediate existential threat as he attempts to steer the Islamic Republic through its most dangerous period since the 1979 revolution.

articles.headline: New Method Claims Million-Fold Speedup in Molecular Sampling
articles.subheadline: Force-guided 'Drifting Models' generate Boltzmann-distributed molecular conformations in a single step, bypassing slow iterative methods
articles.body:
A new computational technique promises to accelerate one of chemistry's most fundamental bottlenecks — sampling molecular conformations from the Boltzmann distribution — by a factor of more than one million compared to traditional molecular dynamics simulations. The method, described in a preprint posted to arXiv by researcher Pipi Hu, introduces force-guided "Drifting Models" that generate physically accurate molecular structures in a single computational step.

Boltzmann sampling underpins much of computational chemistry, from drug design to materials science, by determining the probability of finding a molecule in any given configuration at a specified temperature. Traditional molecular dynamics simulations achieve this by painstakingly simulating atomic motion over many timesteps, while more recent diffusion-based machine learning approaches require hundreds of iterative denoising steps. Both remain computationally expensive for many practical applications.

The new approach builds on Drifting Models — a class of generative models capable of one-step sample generation — and applies them to molecular conformation generation for the first time. The key theoretical contribution is what Hu calls the "Drifting Score Identity," which establishes a mathematical connection between the model's learned drifting field and the score function of the Boltzmann distribution. Because molecular forces directly encode the Boltzmann score (the gradient of the energy landscape), they can be incorporated into the model to correct for deviations between the training data distribution and the true thermodynamic equilibrium.

Intriguingly, the paper reveals that the optimal strategy for incorporating force information depends on how molecular geometry is represented. In Cartesian coordinate space, a technique called Force-Interpolated Drifting — which blends physical force directions with learned data displacements — performs best. In distance-based feature space, however, a different approach called Force-Aligned Kernel, which modifies only statistical weights while preserving geometric validity, proves superior. This representation-dependent reversal is described as a phenomenon unique to molecular systems.

Benchmarked on the MD17 Ethanol dataset, a standard test case in the field, both approaches achieved distributional accuracy comparable to multi-step methods while maintaining perfect structural validity of generated molecules. Relative to recent score-matching techniques with Boltzmann guidance, the method delivered over a thousand-fold speedup, and compared to brute-force molecular dynamics, the acceleration exceeded six orders of magnitude. If these results generalize to larger, more complex molecular systems, the technique could significantly reduce the computational cost of exploring molecular configuration spaces — a persistent bottleneck in pharmaceutical research, protein modeling, and materials discovery.

articles.headline: New 'Q-desic' Equation Suggests Particles Deviate From Einstein's Predicted Paths
articles.subheadline: TU Wien physicists derive quantum corrections to spacetime geodesics that could produce observable effects at cosmic scales
articles.body:
A team of physicists at TU Wien in Vienna has derived a new equation describing how particles move through spacetime when quantum effects are taken into account — and the results suggest that at cosmological distances, those trajectories may diverge significantly from what Einstein's general relativity predicts.

The work, published in Physical Review D, tackles one of the most stubborn problems in fundamental physics: the incompatibility between quantum mechanics, which governs subatomic particles, and general relativity, which describes gravity and the large-scale structure of the universe. Researchers Benjamin Koch, PhD student Ali Riahinia, and Angel Rincón developed a method to quantize the metric — the mathematical object that encodes how spacetime is curved by mass and energy — for the specific case of a spherically symmetric, static gravitational field, such as that produced by the Sun. From this quantized metric, they derived what they call the "q-desic equation," a quantum analog of the classical geodesic equation that determines the shortest path a particle follows through curved spacetime.

The headline result is striking in its scale dependence. Under ordinary gravitational conditions, quantum corrections to particle trajectories amount to roughly 10⁻³⁵ meters — vanishingly small and far beyond any conceivable experimental reach. But when the team incorporated the cosmological constant, the term in Einstein's equations linked to dark energy and the accelerating expansion of the universe, the picture changed dramatically. At distances around 10²¹ meters — comparable to intergalactic scales — the quantum-corrected paths diverged substantially from classical predictions. At intermediate scales, such as planetary orbits, the differences remain negligible. "At first I would not have expected quantum corrections on large scales to produce such dramatic changes," Koch said.

The finding is potentially consequential because it opens a rare window for observational tests of quantum gravity. Physicists have long sought measurable signatures that could distinguish between competing frameworks for unifying quantum mechanics and gravity, including string theory, loop quantum gravity, and asymptotically safe gravity. Koch likened the search to the Cinderella fairy tale: multiple candidate theories exist, but no empirical "glass slipper" has emerged to identify the correct one. The q-desic equation could serve as precisely that kind of discriminator, offering predictions that might eventually be compared against astronomical observations — particularly concerning unresolved puzzles like the anomalous rotation speeds of spiral galaxies, which have traditionally been attributed to dark matter.

The research remains in its early stages. The authors caution that more detailed analysis is needed before the framework can generate precise, testable predictions. But the mere existence of a quantum gravity effect that scales up to observable cosmic distances, rather than remaining confined to the Planck scale, represents a notable development. If further work confirms the robustness of these large-scale deviations, astronomers and cosmologists may have a new tool for probing whether spacetime itself is fundamentally quantum — and which theoretical framework best describes that reality.

articles.headline: Alphabet Awards Sundar Pichai a $692 Million Pay Package Over Three Years
articles.subheadline: Most of the Google CEO's compensation is performance-based, with stock incentives tied to Waymo and Wing milestones
articles.body:
Alphabet has handed CEO Sundar Pichai a three-year compensation package valued at up to $692 million, a deal that would place him among the highest-paid corporate executives in the world. The arrangement, disclosed in a regulatory filing first reported by the Financial Times on March 7, ties the vast majority of the payout to performance benchmarks — including new stock-based incentives linked to the progress of Waymo, Alphabet's autonomous vehicle subsidiary, and Wing, its drone delivery operation.

The structure of the package reflects Alphabet's evolving strategic priorities. By pegging Pichai's compensation to units that sit outside the core advertising business, the company's board appears to be signaling that the long-term value of Alphabet increasingly depends on commercializing its bets in autonomous transportation and logistics. Waymo has been expanding its robotaxi service across multiple U.S. cities, while Wing has quietly built out commercial drone delivery in select markets.

Pichai's wealth has grown substantially since he assumed the CEO role in 2015, a period during which Google's parent company has seen its market capitalization increase nearly sevenfold. He and his wife currently hold Alphabet shares worth close to $500 million, with an estimated $650 million in stock sold as of last summer, according to Bloomberg calculations. Despite his billionaire status, Pichai has maintained a relatively low public profile, remaining based in Los Altos, California.

That stands in contrast to Alphabet co-founders Larry Page and Sergey Brin, the second- and fourth-wealthiest people globally, who have recently drawn attention for purchasing high-end real estate in Miami. Page reportedly spent more than $173 million on two mansions in Coconut Grove, while Brin has been linked to roughly $143 million in Florida property acquisitions. The buying spree is widely interpreted as a hedge against California's proposed Billionaire Tax Act, a ballot initiative that would impose a one-time 5% levy on net worth exceeding $1 billion, targeting the state's approximately 200 billionaires.

Performance-linked pay packages of this scale remain relatively rare but are growing more common among tech companies seeking to retain top leaders during a period of intense competition in artificial intelligence and autonomous systems. Whether Pichai ultimately collects the full amount will depend on whether Alphabet's ambitious subsidiaries can deliver measurable commercial results over the next three years.

articles.headline: Israel Strikes 400 Targets Across Iran in Joint Operation With U.S.
articles.subheadline: Oil refineries, power stations, and Tehran fuel depots hit as retired IDF colonel confirms U.S.-Israel coordination
articles.body:
Israel has struck approximately 400 targets across western and central Iran in a single day of operations, hitting oil refineries, power stations, and fuel infrastructure in what a retired Israeli Defense Forces colonel described as a joint U.S.-Israeli military effort.

Miri Eisin, a retired IDF colonel, confirmed the coordinated nature of the campaign in an interview with Bloomberg Television on March 9, characterizing the strikes as a joint operation between the United States and Israel. The remarks represent one of the most explicit acknowledgments of direct American involvement in offensive operations against Iranian territory, a significant escalation in the broader Middle East conflict.

Israeli military officials said the targets spanned Iran's western and central regions, with strikes concentrated on critical energy and power infrastructure. Iran's semi-official Fars news agency separately reported that Israeli jets targeted oil depots in Tehran and the nearby city of Karaj, suggesting the campaign reached deep into the Iranian capital region. The simultaneous targeting of refineries and electrical generation facilities points to a strategy aimed at degrading Iran's economic capacity and military logistics in a concentrated timeframe.

The scale of the operation — 400 targets in a single day — marks an extraordinary tempo of military activity and suggests extensive pre-planning and intelligence coordination between Washington and Jerusalem. Energy markets are likely to face significant disruption given the direct targeting of Iranian oil infrastructure, which plays a central role in both Iran's domestic economy and global crude supply chains. Iran remains one of OPEC's significant producers, and sustained damage to its refining capacity could ripple through international commodity markets.

The confirmation of U.S. participation transforms the geopolitical calculus in the region. While Washington has historically provided Israel with intelligence, munitions, and diplomatic cover, direct characterization of strikes on Iranian soil as a joint operation raises the stakes for potential Iranian retaliation against American assets and allies across the Middle East. Diplomatic channels, already strained, face an uncertain path forward as both Tehran and the broader international community assess the full extent of the damage and its implications for regional stability.

articles.headline: Oil Retreats From 25% Wartime Spike as G7 Weighs Reserve Release
articles.subheadline: Crude futures pulled back to $103 after surging to $118 as Iran conflict disrupted Strait of Hormuz and Iraqi output
articles.body:
Crude oil futures retreated sharply on Monday after reports that G7 finance ministers are preparing to discuss a coordinated release of emergency petroleum reserves, the most significant such intervention since the 2022 Russia-Ukraine crisis. The move brought prices back from a wartime high of $118 per barrel to approximately $103, still up more than 7% on the day but well below the session's peak.

The pullback followed a dramatic 25% surge that began over the weekend as the conflict between Israel and Iran widened considerably. Iranian missiles struck Saudi Arabia, Israeli strikes escalated into Lebanon, and Iran appointed a new supreme leader. Iraq's oil output plummeted by roughly 60%, while tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — the critical chokepoint through which approximately one-fifth of the world's oil supply passes — effectively collapsed. Three G7 nations, including the United States, have reportedly expressed support for the reserve release plan, and finance ministers are expected to hold a call with International Energy Agency Executive Director Fatih Birol to assess the war's impact on global energy markets.

The price action was particularly visible on crypto-native trading platforms, where tokenized crude oil contracts allowed traders to react to weekend geopolitical developments hours before traditional commodity exchanges opened Monday morning. On Hyperliquid's CL-USDC contract, open interest reached $181.9 million with $823 million in 24-hour trading volume — figures that underscore the growing role of decentralized venues in pricing real-world commodity risk. The platform's oil contracts saw mass liquidations of short positions as prices initially spiked before the G7 headlines triggered the reversal.

The turmoil rippled across Asian financial markets, with Japan's Nikkei 225, South Korea's Kospi, and Taiwan's Taiex all suffering steep declines. Bitcoin, by contrast, steadied near $67,300 after briefly dipping below $66,900, with some analysts noting the cryptocurrency's relative resilience amid an oil-driven equity selloff. Japanese crypto exchange Bitflyer saw trading volumes surge approximately 200%, outpacing gains on global platforms like Coinbase and Binance, as investors sought alternatives to tumbling equities.

Whether the proposed G7 intervention can meaningfully offset the supply disruption remains an open question. The effectiveness of any coordinated reserve release will depend on its scale and duration — and critically, on how long the Strait of Hormuz remains functionally closed to commercial shipping. If the conflict continues to escalate, even a large-scale drawdown of strategic reserves may prove insufficient to stabilize markets that are pricing in the possibility of a prolonged disruption to one of the world's most vital energy corridors.

articles.headline: New Ultrasound Technique Delivers Molecules Into Cells Without Chemicals
articles.subheadline: Researchers develop programmable acoustic standing-wave system that opens temporary pores in cell membranes with high viability
articles.body:
A team of researchers has demonstrated a microfluidic platform that uses dynamically programmable ultrasonic fields to shuttle biomolecules directly into living cells — bypassing the chemical carriers and viral vectors that have long complicated intracellular delivery in both laboratory research and therapeutic development.

The technique, called Programmable Acoustic Standing-wave Transfection (PAST), was detailed in a paper posted to the arXiv preprint server on March 4 by Subhas Nandy, Monica Manohar, and Ashis K. Sen. The system generates tunable acoustic potential landscapes that subject cell clusters to carefully controlled cycles of hydrodynamic and acoustic stress. These stresses transiently open pores in cell membranes, allowing biomolecules to diffuse inward without the need for contrast agents or chemical permeabilization — processes that often compromise cell health or introduce unwanted side effects.

What distinguishes PAST from earlier sonoporation approaches is the degree of programmability the researchers achieved. By modulating acoustic power, frequency, and duty cycles, the team demonstrated fine-grained control over both the influx and efflux of multiple biomolecular species across cell membranes. Theoretical scaling laws and numerical simulations developed alongside the experiments showed that membrane tension, pore energetics, and acoustic field geometry collectively govern transmembrane transport — and that effective diffusivity estimates from their models closely matched observed transport timescales, lending quantitative credibility to the framework.

Critically, post-treatment assays confirmed that cells maintained high viability and continued to proliferate normally after exposure to the acoustic fields, addressing one of the central concerns in any membrane permeabilization strategy. The reversibility of pore formation appears to be key: the acoustic stresses are sufficient to open transient pathways but not so intense as to cause irreversible damage.

The implications extend across multiple domains. In drug screening, PAST could enable rapid, carrier-free loading of candidate compounds into cells at scale. In gene editing, it offers a physical delivery mechanism that avoids the immunogenicity risks associated with viral vectors. And in fundamental biophysics, the platform provides a new experimental handle for probing how cellular membranes respond to mechanical perturbation. The 35-page paper, which has not yet undergone peer review, positions PAST as a high-throughput, non-invasive alternative in a field that has long struggled to balance delivery efficiency with cell survival.

articles.headline: Saudi Aramco Makes Rare Spot Market Oil Offer as Hormuz Closure Reroutes Global Flows
articles.subheadline: The world's largest crude exporter is tendering 4.6 million barrels across three grades as war with Iran chokes a vital shipping lane
articles.body:
Saudi Aramco has taken the unusual step of offering crude oil directly on the spot market through a series of emergency tenders, as the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz — the world's most critical oil chokepoint — disrupts contracted supply flows and forces a sweeping reroute of shipments through the Red Sea.

The state oil giant has offered approximately 4.6 million barrels across three grades — Arab Extra Light, Arab Heavy, and its flagship Arab Light — according to traders familiar with the transactions, as reported by Bloomberg on Sunday. The move is rare for Aramco, which typically sells the vast majority of its crude through long-term contracts with fixed customers rather than open market tenders. The shift signals the depth of logistical disruption caused by the ongoing military conflict between Saudi Arabia's regional adversary Iran and forces operating in and around the strait.

The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman, normally handles roughly 20 percent of the world's daily oil consumption. Its effective closure has trapped tankers and severed established shipping routes, compelling Saudi Arabia to redirect exports through alternative infrastructure, including pipelines to Red Sea terminals. While the kingdom possesses significant pipeline capacity to bypass Hormuz — notably the East-West pipeline to the Yanbu port on the Red Sea — the logistical reconfiguration has disrupted the timing and allocation of contracted deliveries, creating a need to offload barrels through spot sales.

The spot tenders come at a moment of extreme volatility in global energy markets. Oil prices have surged since the outbreak of hostilities, with traders pricing in both the physical loss of Hormuz transit capacity and the risk of further escalation. The 4.6 million barrels on offer represent a relatively modest volume compared to Saudi Arabia's total production capacity, but the signal it sends — that even the world's most disciplined oil exporter is resorting to ad hoc sales mechanisms — underscores the severity of the supply chain breakdown.

For global buyers scrambling to secure barrels, the Aramco tenders offer a rare opportunity to purchase Saudi crude outside the rigid contract system. But analysts warn that the rerouting of flows through the Red Sea introduces its own risks, given the region's history of Houthi attacks on commercial shipping. The broader question facing markets is how long the Hormuz disruption will persist and whether alternative export routes can sustain anything close to normal volumes.

articles.headline: Oil Prices Breach $100 a Barrel as Iran Conflict Enters Second Week
articles.subheadline: Supply disruption fears drive crude to highest levels in years as Middle East war intensifies market volatility
articles.body:
Crude oil prices surged past the $100-per-barrel threshold on Sunday as the military conflict involving Iran moved into its second week, amplifying fears of prolonged supply disruptions across one of the world's most critical energy-producing regions.

The breach of the psychologically significant $100 mark represents the most dramatic price spike in years, driven by mounting concerns that the widening conflict could choke off oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily petroleum consumption passes. Iran, itself a major crude producer and OPEC member, sits directly on the strait's northern shore, giving the conflict immediate and outsized implications for global energy markets.

The rally has sent shockwaves through commodity trading desks and central bank forecasting units alike. A sustained period above $100 would threaten to reignite inflationary pressures that policymakers in the United States, Europe, and Asia have spent years attempting to bring under control. For consumers, the surge portends higher gasoline prices, elevated heating costs, and a potential drag on economic growth at a time when many major economies remain on uncertain footing.

Energy analysts have warned that the risk premium baked into crude prices could climb further if the conflict escalates or if Iranian oil infrastructure sustains damage. Even a partial disruption of Iranian exports — which have fluctuated in recent years due to sanctions — would tighten an already finely balanced global supply picture. Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates hold some spare production capacity, but market participants remain skeptical that any offsetting increase could fully compensate for a major regional disruption.

The geopolitical premium now embedded in oil markets is also reverberating across equities, with energy stocks rallying while transport and airline shares have come under pressure. Bond markets, meanwhile, are repricing inflation expectations upward. As the conflict enters its second week with no clear path to de-escalation, traders and policymakers are bracing for an extended period of elevated energy costs and the economic turbulence that typically follows.

articles.headline: AI Drug Discovery Tool Boltz-2 Falls Short on Binding Accuracy
articles.subheadline: Large-scale evaluation of 38,000 compounds reveals AI model lacks precision needed for lead drug identification
articles.body:
A rigorous evaluation of Boltz-2, one of the newest AI-powered tools for drug discovery, has found that the model fails to deliver the energetic precision necessary for identifying promising drug candidates — adding fresh evidence to a growing debate about the reliability of artificial intelligence in pharmaceutical research.

Researchers led by Peter Coveney at University College London tested Boltz-2 against two large-scale datasets comprising 16,780 compounds targeting the SARS-CoV-2 main protease (3CLPro) and 21,702 compounds for tankyrase 2 (TNKS2), a cancer-related enzyme. The biomolecular foundation model, which uses a "co-folding" approach to simultaneously predict protein-ligand structures and binding affinities, was benchmarked against traditional molecular docking methods and physics-based binding free energy calculations using the established ESMACS protocol. The results, published on the arXiv preprint server on March 2, paint a sobering picture for AI enthusiasts in the pharmaceutical space.

Structural analysis revealed that Boltz-2 produced significant variations in global root-mean-square deviation (RMSD), indicating that rather than converging on a single reliable binding pose, the model predicted multiple protein conformations and ligand positions. When the team examined energetic predictions across the full datasets, they found only weak to moderate correlations with physics-based free energy calculations. Perhaps most damaging was a focused analysis of the top 100 compounds in each dataset: the researchers observed no significant correlation between Boltz-2's predictions and the fine-grained ESMACS binding free energies, along with problematic saturation in predicted ligand structures.

The findings underscore a persistent gap in AI-driven drug discovery. As the authors pointedly note, no "AI-discovered drugs" have yet received regulatory approval despite years of intense hype and billions of dollars in investment. While Boltz-2 offers meaningful speed advantages for initial compound screening — processing tens of thousands of molecules far faster than physics-based simulations — that speed comes at the cost of the energetic resolution required to reliably distinguish true drug leads from false positives.

The study by Shunzhou Wan, Xibei Zhang, Xiao Xue, and Coveney reinforces what many computational chemists have argued: that physics-based methods remain essential for validating and refining AI-generated predictions. Rather than replacing traditional approaches, current AI tools may be best suited as a rapid first filter in multi-stage drug discovery pipelines, with rigorous physics-based calculations still needed to make the critical decisions about which compounds advance toward clinical development.

articles.headline: Oil Prices Surge as Iran Tensions Escalate, Bond Selloff Intensifies
articles.subheadline: Geopolitical turmoil surrounding Iran drives crude higher while fixed-income markets face deepening pressure
articles.body:
Oil prices climbed sharply and a global bond selloff deepened on Saturday as escalating tensions involving Iran rattled financial markets and raised the specter of broader conflict in the Middle East.

Crude benchmarks moved higher as traders priced in the risk of supply disruptions from a region that accounts for roughly a third of the world's seaborne oil trade. Iran, an OPEC member producing approximately 3.2 million barrels per day, sits along the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow chokepoint through which roughly 20% of global petroleum consumption flows daily. Any military escalation threatening that corridor has historically triggered sharp spikes in energy prices and sent investors scrambling for safe-haven assets.

The bond market reaction, however, complicated the traditional flight-to-safety playbook. Rather than rallying on geopolitical fear, government bonds sold off further, pushing yields higher. The deepening selloff suggests that investors are weighing the inflationary consequences of surging energy costs against the economic drag of prolonged conflict. Higher oil prices feed directly into consumer costs, potentially forcing central banks to maintain restrictive monetary policy even as growth risks mount — a stagflationary combination that is particularly punishing for fixed-income portfolios.

The dual move — rising oil and falling bond prices — underscores the difficult positioning facing global portfolio managers. Equities face headwinds from both higher input costs and rising discount rates, while traditional hedges like sovereign debt are failing to provide the diversification benefits investors have come to expect. Gold and other hard commodities could emerge as primary beneficiaries if the situation continues to deteriorate.

Market participants will be closely monitoring diplomatic channels and military developments in the coming days for signals on whether the current escalation represents a contained episode or the opening phase of a broader conflict. Energy analysts have warned that sustained disruption to Iranian output or Strait of Hormuz transit could push Brent crude well above $100 per barrel, a threshold that would ripple through global economies already grappling with sticky inflation and uneven growth. Central bank officials in Washington, Frankfurt, and London face the unenviable task of calibrating monetary policy against a backdrop where geopolitical risk is rapidly repricing the outlook for both growth and prices.

articles.headline: Crude Oil Surges 30%, Crushing $37M in Crypto Short Positions
articles.subheadline: Iran-Israel conflict escalation triggers historic oil spike and massive liquidations on Hyperliquid's tokenized commodity contracts
articles.body:
A dramatic escalation in the Iran-Israel conflict over the weekend sent crude oil prices soaring roughly 30% — potentially the largest single-day percentage gain in the history of the oil market — and obliterated nearly $40 million worth of short positions on crypto-based tokenized oil contracts.

The carnage was concentrated on Hyperliquid, a decentralized perpetual futures exchange that has become a go-to venue for traders seeking leveraged commodity exposure outside traditional market hours. Of the $40 million in total oil liquidations recorded by data provider CoinGlass over the past 24 hours, approximately $36.9 million came from short sellers — traders betting on lower prices who were catastrophically wrong. Hyperliquid's CL-USDC contract surged to $114.77, up nearly 20% in a single day, while its USOIL-USDH pair hit $135. Brent and WTI benchmarks reached levels not seen since Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

The geopolitical trigger was a weekend of rapidly compounding crises. Iran appointed Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader after his father was killed in an opening wave of Israeli strikes. Israel followed with fresh attacks on Iranian and Hezbollah infrastructure, prompting Iranian retaliatory missile and drone strikes that expanded beyond Israel to hit targets in Saudi Arabia and Bahrain, including energy infrastructure near Riyadh. Iraq's oil output reportedly collapsed by roughly 60%, while Kuwait and the UAE curtailed production as tanker traffic through the strategically vital Strait of Hormuz ground to a near halt.

The episode underscored the growing role of crypto perpetual markets as venues for expressing macroeconomic views on traditional commodities. Hyperliquid's CL-USDC contract carried $195 million in open interest and $570 million in 24-hour trading volume — figures that would have been nearly unimaginable for a tokenized commodity product just a year ago. The platform's 24/7 availability and lower margin requirements have made it one of the few places in the world where traders can access leveraged crude exposure when geopolitical events unfold on weekends, outside traditional exchange hours.

The broader crypto market did not escape unscathed. CoinGlass data showed 94,058 traders liquidated across all assets in 24 hours, with total losses reaching $364.4 million. Bitcoin accounted for $156.67 million, Ethereum added $70.88 million, and Solana contributed $19.8 million. Notably, long liquidations outpaced shorts at $215 million versus $149 million, reflecting a broader risk-off selloff as investors fled speculative assets. Bitcoin itself traded near $67,000, showing relative stability compared to the volatility rippling through commodity-linked products, a dynamic analysts attributed in part to the United States' status as a net oil exporter insulating its markets from the worst of the energy shock.

articles.headline: Dollar Surges as Middle East Conflict Sends Oil Past $100 a Barrel
articles.subheadline: Escalating Iran war drives investors into haven assets, rippling across global currency and commodity markets
articles.body:
The U.S. dollar rallied broadly against major currencies on Saturday as an intensifying military conflict involving Iran sent shockwaves through global markets, pushing crude oil prices above the $100-per-barrel threshold for the first time in years.

The greenback's strength reflects a classic flight-to-safety pattern, with investors scrambling for haven assets amid deepening uncertainty in the Middle East. The surge in oil prices — driven by fears of supply disruptions from one of the world's most critical energy-producing regions — has further bolstered the dollar, as commodity markets price in the risk of a prolonged conflict that could reshape global energy flows.

The breach of the $100 oil mark represents a significant psychological and economic milestone. Sustained prices at that level would exert fresh inflationary pressure on economies worldwide, complicating the policy calculus for central banks that have spent the past several years battling elevated consumer prices. For the United States, higher energy costs could slow consumer spending, while the dollar's appreciation may partially offset import price increases.

Currency markets bore the immediate brunt of the risk repricing. Commodity-importing nations' currencies weakened against the dollar, while traditional safe-haven assets including gold and U.S. Treasuries also saw increased demand. The moves underscore how rapidly geopolitical shocks can cascade through interconnected financial systems, forcing portfolio managers to reassess risk exposures across asset classes.

The trajectory of the conflict will likely determine whether the current market dislocations deepen or stabilize. Analysts warn that any further escalation — particularly if it threatens oil transit routes through the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's petroleum passes — could send energy prices significantly higher and intensify the dollar's haven bid. For now, traders are bracing for continued volatility as the geopolitical situation remains fluid.

articles.headline: Oil Surges Past $100 as Strait of Hormuz Closure Roils Energy Markets
articles.subheadline: Middle East producers cut output amid Iran conflict as U.S. signals possible escalation of military involvement
articles.body:
Crude oil prices surpassed $100 per barrel on March 8 as the ongoing conflict involving Iran effectively shuttered the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's most critical energy chokepoints, and prompted additional production cuts from major Middle East producers.

The price spike marks a dramatic escalation in energy market disruption driven by the widening military conflict in the Persian Gulf region. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption flows, has been rendered nearly impassable, choking off a supply artery that connects Gulf producers to global markets. With multiple major producers now curtailing output on top of the physical blockage, analysts warn that the supply shock could deepen if hostilities continue to intensify.

Compounding the crisis, the United States has signaled its willingness to expand its role in the conflict, raising the prospect of a broader regional war that could further destabilize production and shipping infrastructure across the Gulf. Washington's posture has injected additional uncertainty into a market already grappling with the most severe supply disruption in years. The combination of lost barrels from production cuts and the near-total closure of the strait has left traders scrambling to secure alternative supplies and reroute cargoes.

The breach of the $100 threshold carries significant implications for the global economy. Sustained triple-digit oil prices would accelerate inflationary pressures that central banks in the U.S., Europe, and Asia have spent years attempting to tame. Energy-importing nations face the sharpest cost increases, while consumers worldwide are likely to see the impact at the pump within days. Strategic petroleum reserves may come under political pressure for release, though such measures would offer only temporary relief against a structural supply deficit.

Market participants are now closely watching diplomatic channels for any sign of de-escalation that could reopen the strait and restore production flows. Absent a ceasefire or negotiated settlement, traders and analysts expect prices to remain elevated and potentially climb further, particularly if U.S. military engagement deepens or additional infrastructure in the region sustains damage.

articles.headline: Machine Learning Meets Quantum Chemistry in New Density Matrix Method
articles.subheadline: Researchers combine neural networks with semidefinite programming to calculate molecular electronic structures more accurately
articles.body:
A pair of researchers at the University of Chicago have developed a novel computational framework that fuses machine learning with established quantum chemistry techniques, potentially offering a more efficient route to accurate molecular electronic structure calculations.

Luis H. Delgado-Granados and David A. Mazziotti describe their "semidefinite machine learning" approach in a preprint posted to arXiv, detailing how an input convex neural network can be paired with semidefinite programming to directly compute two-electron reduced density matrices (2-RDMs) — mathematical objects that encode the essential quantum mechanical information about how electrons interact in molecules. The 2-RDM approach has long attracted interest because it bypasses the need to solve for a full many-electron wavefunction, but its practical accuracy has been limited by the difficulty of enforcing so-called N-representability conditions, which ensure the density matrix corresponds to a physically valid quantum state.

Traditionally, enforcing these conditions requires constructing linear matrix inequalities of increasing order, with higher-order constraints improving accuracy but dramatically escalating computational cost. The new method sidesteps this scaling problem by training a neural network on molecular data to learn a vertex-based approximation of the boundary of the allowed 2-RDM set. This data-driven boundary information is then woven into a semidefinite optimization that achieves accuracy beyond what standard low-order positivity constraints alone can deliver — without ever explicitly constructing the expensive higher-order conditions.

The researchers demonstrated their approach on the potential energy curves of three isoelectronic molecular species: the carbon dianion C₂²⁻, molecular nitrogen N₂, and the oxygen dication O₂²⁺. In each case, the semidefinite machine learning results showed systematic improvements over conventional two-positivity calculations and closely matched results from complete active space configuration interaction, a well-established high-accuracy benchmark method. Critically, the computational cost remained comparable to the simpler two-positivity approach.

The work sits at a productive intersection of quantum physics and artificial intelligence that has seen growing activity in recent years. By learning the geometric structure of the allowed density matrix space from data rather than deriving it analytically, the method suggests a broader paradigm in which machine learning could help circumvent longstanding computational bottlenecks in quantum chemistry. If the approach generalizes to larger and more complex molecular systems, it could accelerate the study of strongly correlated electron systems — a domain relevant to catalysis, materials design, and high-temperature superconductivity — where traditional methods often struggle.

articles.headline: Oil Surges Past $110 as Iran Conflict Chokes Strait of Hormuz
articles.subheadline: Brent crude jumps 24% in early Asian trading as week-old US-Israeli war with Iran halts Gulf shipping and rattles global markets
articles.body:
Global oil prices rocketed above $110 a barrel on Monday as the escalating US-Israeli military conflict with Iran effectively shut down shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's oil supply normally transits. The price shock sent stock markets across Asia into freefall and raised the specter of a broader global inflation crisis.

Brent crude surged nearly 24% to $114.74 a barrel, while the U.S. benchmark Nymex light sweet climbed more than 26% to $114.78 — the first time either contract has traded above $100 since 2021. The move was breathtakingly fast: oil jumped 10% in the first minute of Asian trading, then added another 10% within fifteen minutes. Fresh waves of U.S. and Israeli airstrikes over the weekend struck Iranian oil depots and energy infrastructure, deepening fears that the week-old conflict will cause prolonged disruption to global energy supplies.

Asian equity markets absorbed the blow harshly. Japan's Nikkei 225 plunged more than 7%, Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell over 3%, and Australia's ASX 200 dropped more than 4%. South Korea's Kospi index suffered the worst damage, sinking more than 8% and triggering a 20-minute circuit-breaker halt — the second such emergency pause in less than a week after the index crashed 12% on Wednesday. Traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has "all but halted" since the conflict began, trapping millions of barrels of crude and liquefied natural gas in the Persian Gulf.

The geopolitical picture darkened further on Sunday when Iran named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as Supreme Leader, signaling that hardliners remain firmly in control. Adnan Mazarei of the Peterson Institute for International Economics said the oil price spike was predictable given halted Gulf production and signs of a prolonged war. "People are realizing that this won't end quickly," he said, adding that U.S. assurances about limited objectives are "becoming more unrealistic." Some analysts warn that if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed through the end of March, oil could breach $150 a barrel — a record that would cascade into higher costs for jet fuel, fertilizers, and consumer goods worldwide.

U.S. President Donald Trump dismissed the price surge as a "small price to pay" for neutralizing Iran's nuclear threat, while his energy secretary sought to distance Washington from the destruction of Iranian energy infrastructure, attributing those strikes to Israel. Already, market dynamics are shifting: Asian buyers have begun bidding up prices for American natural gas, with some LNG tankers originally bound for Europe reportedly reversing course in the mid-Atlantic. The physical reordering of global energy flows, analysts say, could persist well beyond the conflict itself.

articles.headline: Global Bond Markets Slide as Surging Oil Prices Revive Stagflation Fears
articles.subheadline: Monday's selloff across Asian trading sessions signals growing investor anxiety over simultaneous inflation and growth risks
articles.body:
A sharp selloff swept through global bond markets during Monday's Asian trading session, as a sudden surge in oil prices forced investors to reassess the twin threats of rising inflation and slowing economic growth — a combination that has historically proved toxic for fixed-income portfolios.

The rout, which gathered momentum throughout early Asian hours on March 9, reflects deepening concern that an oil price shock could tip major economies toward stagflation, the feared economic condition in which persistent inflation coincides with stagnant output. Bond yields, which move inversely to prices, climbed as traders unwound positions and repriced expectations for central bank policy paths that had previously assumed a more benign inflation trajectory.

The selloff underscores a fundamental dilemma now confronting policymakers at the Federal Reserve, European Central Bank, and other major institutions. Rising energy costs feed directly into consumer prices, which would typically call for tighter monetary policy. But the same price shock acts as a drag on economic activity by squeezing household budgets and corporate margins, arguing for looser conditions. Central banks caught between these opposing forces have historically struggled to chart a course that avoids deepening either problem.

For investors, the repricing carries immediate portfolio consequences. Government bonds, traditionally viewed as safe havens during periods of economic uncertainty, lose their protective appeal when inflation erodes real returns. The simultaneous deterioration in growth expectations also weighs on risk assets, leaving few places to hide. Oil-sensitive sectors and import-dependent economies in Asia appeared particularly exposed as markets opened to start the week.

The episode serves as a reminder of how quickly energy market disruptions can reshape the global macro landscape. With bond markets already navigating elevated government borrowing levels across developed economies, the additional pressure from an oil-driven inflation scare threatens to amplify volatility in the weeks ahead. Traders will be closely watching how crude prices develop and whether central bank officials signal any shift in their policy calculus in response to the changed outlook.

articles.headline: Oil Futures Spike Past $110 as Middle East Crisis Roils Global Markets
articles.subheadline: Asian equities suffer steep losses while bitcoin holds near $67,000, signaling crypto decoupling from broader risk-off trade
articles.body:
Crude oil prices erupted past $110 a barrel on Monday as escalating military conflict in the Middle East stoked fears of a major supply disruption, triggering sharp selloffs across Asian equity markets while cryptocurrency markets remained notably calm.

West Texas Intermediate crude surged approximately 17 percent in just 24 hours, driven by concerns that fighting near the Strait of Hormuz — the narrow waterway through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily crude supply transits — could choke off critical oil flows. The price shock cascaded immediately into equity markets across Asia, where import-dependent economies face outsized exposure to energy costs. Japan's Nikkei 225 plunged more than 6 percent, while South Korea's Kospi suffered an even steeper decline of roughly 8 percent, with every major regional index opening deep in the red.

Prediction markets suggest the worst may not be over for energy prices. On Polymarket, traders assigned a 76 percent probability that crude oil will reach $120 a barrel before the end of March, reflecting deep unease about the trajectory of the conflict. Yet not all participants are convinced the rally will hold. Funding rates on oil perpetual futures turned negative on the decentralized exchange Hyperliquid, indicating that a significant cohort of traders is positioning for a pullback even as spot prices climb — a tension that could fuel sharp volatility in either direction in the days ahead.

Bitcoin, meanwhile, held steady near $67,000 with little evidence of panic selling. Ether and Solana posted modest gains, suggesting that digital asset markets have so far interpreted the turmoil as an energy-specific shock rather than a catalyst for broad risk aversion. The resilience marks a departure from previous geopolitical crises, where crypto typically moved in lockstep with risk assets.

The oil spike carries significant implications for monetary policy. A sustained rally in crude would reignite inflationary pressures just as the Federal Reserve weighs its next move. Polymarket contracts show a 98 percent probability that the Fed will hold rates steady at its March 18 meeting, with only about a 12 percent chance of a 25-basis-point cut by the end of April. Should energy prices remain elevated, the central bank's already narrow path to easing could narrow further, leaving markets caught between geopolitical risk and persistently tight financial conditions.

articles.headline: Global Stocks Tumble as Oil Breaches $100 Amid Iran Conflict
articles.subheadline: Crude hits highest level since 2022 as US-Israeli military campaign against Iran shows no signs of abating
articles.body:
Global equity markets sold off sharply on Monday as crude oil prices surged past $100 per barrel for the first time in nearly four years, driven by escalating hostilities between the US-Israeli coalition and Iran that show no indication of nearing resolution.

The broad-based decline in stocks reflected deepening investor anxiety over the geopolitical crisis and its cascading economic consequences. The spike in oil prices — a key input cost across virtually every sector of the global economy — reignited fears of persistent inflation just as central banks had been making progress in taming price pressures. The U.S. dollar strengthened on safe-haven flows, while Treasury prices fell across the yield curve, pushing borrowing costs higher and compounding the pain for risk assets.

Crude oil's breach of the $100 threshold marks a significant psychological milestone for energy markets. Prices had not reached that level since 2022, when the initial shock of Russia's invasion of Ukraine sent commodities spiraling. The current conflict between the US-Israeli alliance and Iran — which controls critical oil infrastructure and sits along vital shipping lanes near the Strait of Hormuz — has injected a substantial risk premium into global energy pricing. With neither side signaling willingness to de-escalate, traders are bracing for a sustained period of elevated prices.

The market turmoil underscores how quickly geopolitical shocks can upend the economic outlook. Higher energy costs threaten to squeeze corporate margins, dampen consumer spending, and complicate the calculus for the Federal Reserve and other central banks attempting to balance inflation control with growth. Rising Treasury yields add further headwinds for equity valuations, particularly in rate-sensitive sectors such as technology and real estate.

Analysts warned that markets could face extended volatility if the conflict widens or disrupts physical oil supply chains. Any move by Iran to restrict tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz — through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption passes — would likely push crude prices significantly higher still, deepening the economic fallout well beyond financial markets.

articles.headline: Physicists Discover New 'Island of Inversion' in Symmetric Nucleus
articles.subheadline: Molybdenum-84, with equal protons and neutrons, defies decades of nuclear physics assumptions about exotic deformation
articles.body:
An international team of physicists has identified a fundamentally new type of structural anomaly in the atomic nucleus — an "Island of Inversion" in molybdenum-84, a nucleus with perfectly equal numbers of protons and neutrons. The discovery, published in Nature Communications, upends the long-held belief that such exotic nuclear behavior occurs only in neutron-rich isotopes far from stability.

Islands of Inversion are regions of the nuclear chart where atomic nuclei abandon their expected orderly shell structure and warp into highly deformed shapes. Previously, every known example — including beryllium-12, magnesium-32, and chromium-64 — involved nuclei loaded with excess neutrons. The new finding by researchers from the Institute for Basic Science (IBS) in South Korea, the University of Padova, Michigan State University, the University of Strasbourg, and other institutions reveals that molybdenum-84, sitting on the symmetric N = Z line with 42 protons and 42 neutrons, undergoes the same dramatic structural collapse.

The team used rare isotope beams at Michigan State University, accelerating molybdenum-92 ions into a beryllium target to produce molybdenum-86 nuclei. A secondary collision then generated molybdenum-84 by knocking out two neutrons. Using GRETINA, a high-resolution germanium detector array, and TRIPLEX, an instrument capable of measuring nuclear lifetimes on picosecond timescales, the researchers tracked gamma rays emitted as the nuclei relaxed to their ground states. The data revealed a stark contrast: despite differing by just two neutrons, molybdenum-84 and molybdenum-86 behave in fundamentally different ways. Molybdenum-84 exhibits massive collective motion — an 8-particle-8-hole excitation in which nucleons leap across a major shell gap in coordinated fashion, producing extreme deformation. Molybdenum-86, by comparison, shows more modest 4-particle-4-hole excitations and remains far less distorted.

Theoretical analysis showed that the dramatic behavior of molybdenum-84 arises from the interplay between proton-neutron symmetry and a narrowing of the nuclear shell gap at N = Z = 40. Crucially, the researchers found that reproducing the observed structure required accounting for three-nucleon forces — interactions in which three particles influence one another simultaneously. Traditional models relying solely on two-body nuclear forces could not explain the data.

The discovery of what the team calls an "Isospin-Symmetric Island of Inversion" marks the first time such a region has been identified in a proton-neutron balanced system. It challenges foundational assumptions about the boundaries of nuclear shell structure and opens a new frontier for understanding the forces that bind matter at its most fundamental level. The work suggests that the nuclear landscape may harbor additional surprises in symmetric regions that were previously considered well understood.

articles.headline: Columbia Physicists Control Superconductivity With Built-In Light Cavity
articles.subheadline: First-ever demonstration shows quantum materials can be engineered to alter superconducting properties without external fields or pressure
articles.body:
A team of physicists at Columbia University has achieved what was previously only theorized: altering a material's superconducting behavior by coupling it to an integrated light-confining cavity, with no external light source, magnetic field, or pressure required. The results, published in Nature, mark a significant advance in the emerging field of cavity quantum materials engineering.

Led by Itai Keren, the Columbia researchers demonstrated that by bonding carefully selected materials together, they could construct a system in which a built-in optical cavity directly modifies the quantum properties governing superconductivity. The approach is notable for its passivity — rather than bombarding a sample with lasers or subjecting it to extreme external conditions, the team engineered the desired quantum coupling into the material stack itself. The cavity confines virtual photons, whose quantum fluctuations interact with the electrons responsible for superconducting behavior, effectively tuning the material's properties from within.

The experiment sits at the intersection of two rapidly advancing fields: cavity quantum electrodynamics, which studies how confined electromagnetic fields interact with matter, and condensed matter physics, where researchers have long sought new levers to control exotic quantum phases like superconductivity. Previous work had shown that intense external laser light could transiently modify material properties, but the Columbia result demonstrates that permanent, structural coupling to a photonic environment can achieve analogous effects in equilibrium — a far more practical and stable configuration.

The implications extend well beyond a single demonstration. If superconductivity can be deliberately engineered through material architecture alone, it opens a design paradigm in which quantum phases are selected and tuned at the fabrication stage rather than maintained through costly and complex external apparatus. This could eventually influence the development of quantum computing components, ultra-sensitive detectors, and novel electronic devices that exploit superconducting states.

While the work represents a proof of principle rather than an immediately scalable technology, it provides the first experimental confirmation that vacuum electromagnetic environments — cavities with no photons deliberately injected — can reshape a material's macroscopic quantum behavior. The finding is expected to catalyze further research into cavity-engineered quantum materials, a field that until now has been driven largely by theoretical predictions.

articles.headline: Bitcoin Slides Below $66,000 as Oil Prices Surge Nearly 20%
articles.subheadline: Escalating U.S.-Iran conflict sends crude to four-year highs and rattles global risk assets across the board
articles.body:
Bitcoin dropped sharply on Sunday evening as a nearly 20% spike in oil prices rippled through global markets, underscoring the cryptocurrency's vulnerability to geopolitical shocks even as proponents tout its role as a hedge against traditional financial turmoil.

The world's largest cryptocurrency fell approximately 2% to just under $66,000 as April West Texas Intermediate crude oil futures surged 19.1% to $108.35 per barrel — roughly double where oil started 2026 and the highest level in about four years. The catalyst was a weekend that offered no signs of de-escalation in the ongoing U.S. military conflict with Iran, prompting traders to price in sustained disruption to Middle Eastern energy supplies. Ether and Solana also declined, losing around 1.4% each.

The damage extended well beyond crypto. U.S. stock index futures fell nearly 2% across the board, while Japan's Nikkei 225 futures dropped 3.1% ahead of Monday's Asian session open. Precious metals and copper, often considered safe havens or inflation hedges, traded modestly lower as well — suggesting that the initial market reaction was a broad flight from risk rather than a rotation into alternative stores of value.

The sell-off fits a pattern that has become grimly familiar in 2026. Despite bitcoin's narrative as "digital gold" and growing institutional interest — including ongoing debate about its potential as a sovereign reserve asset — the cryptocurrency has repeatedly traded in lockstep with equities during periods of acute geopolitical stress. Analysts have noted that bitcoin's correlation with major stock indexes remains elevated near 0.5, meaning equities explain roughly a quarter of its price movements. That leaves room for independent drivers, but in moments of panic, risk-off sentiment tends to overwhelm bitcoin's diversification case.

The immediate question for markets is whether the Iran conflict shows any prospect of containment. With oil now trading above $100 per barrel for cargoes leaving the Middle East, the inflationary implications are significant — potentially complicating any plans by the Federal Reserve to ease monetary policy and adding another headwind for risk assets. For bitcoin, which posted its best week of Wall Street-related news in months just days ago yet still failed to hold the $70,000 level, the geopolitical backdrop presents a formidable obstacle to any sustained recovery. On-chain data showing whale selling into retail buying has added to bearish sentiment, suggesting that larger holders may be positioning defensively ahead of further turbulence.

articles.headline: Iran Appoints Khamenei's Son as Supreme Leader Amid Expanding Regional War
articles.subheadline: Mojtaba Khamenei's elevation marks a dynastic succession as Tehran continues strikes across multiple countries on day nine of conflict
articles.body:
Iran has installed Mojtaba Khamenei, son of the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as the country's new supreme leader, consolidating power within the founding family of the Islamic Republic even as the nation wages an expanding military campaign across the Middle East.

The appointment, reported by Bloomberg on March 8, came on the ninth day of a widening regional war that has seen Tehran launch strikes against multiple countries. The selection of the younger Khamenei — long rumored to wield significant behind-the-scenes influence over Iran's security apparatus — represents a striking dynastic turn for a theocratic republic that was born from the overthrow of the Pahlavi monarchy in 1979. The elder Khamenei, who had served as supreme leader since 1989, wielded ultimate authority over Iran's military, judiciary, and foreign policy for more than three decades.

The succession arrives at a moment of extraordinary peril for the region. Nine days of sustained hostilities have drawn in several nations, though the full scope of combatants and the precise triggers of the conflict remain fluid. Iran's continued offensive posture under new leadership suggests no immediate prospect of de-escalation, and the elevation of Mojtaba Khamenei signals that Tehran's hardline establishment intends to maintain strategic continuity even as the battlefield situation evolves.

Mojtaba Khamenei, believed to be in his mid-50s, has long operated in the shadows of Iranian power. Western intelligence assessments and Iranian exile groups have for years described him as a key node connecting the supreme leader's office to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps and the country's vast network of proxy forces across the Middle East. His formal ascension removes any ambiguity about his role and places him atop a nation actively engaged in multi-front warfare.

The developments are reverberating through global energy and financial markets, with the Middle East conflict threatening critical oil infrastructure and shipping routes. Diplomatic channels appear strained, and international calls for a ceasefire have so far gained little traction. How the newly installed supreme leader navigates the coming days — whether he seeks off-ramps or doubles down on confrontation — could define the trajectory of the most significant Middle Eastern conflict in decades.

articles.headline: Organized Fraud Networks Now Outpace Legitimate Science Publishing
articles.subheadline: Northwestern University study reveals global 'paper mill' operations selling fake research, authorships, and citations at industrial scale
articles.body:
Fraudulent scientific papers are now being produced at a faster rate than legitimate research, according to a sweeping investigation published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences that exposes a global underground economy built on fabricated data, purchased authorships, and compromised academic journals.

The study, led by Luís A. N. Amaral and first author Reese Richardson at Northwestern University's McCormick School of Engineering, analyzed massive datasets spanning major scientific databases — including Web of Science, Scopus, PubMed, and OpenAlex — along with retraction records from Retraction Watch and discussion threads from PubPeer. What emerged was not a picture of isolated bad actors cutting corners, but a portrait of coordinated criminal enterprises. So-called "paper mills" function as production lines, churning out manuscripts containing fabricated data, manipulated images, and plagiarized text, then selling authorship slots for hundreds or thousands of dollars depending on position. Brokers connect writers, paying "authors," compliant editors, and compromised journals into a seamless pipeline. "These networks are essentially criminal organizations, acting together to fake the process of science," Amaral said. "Millions of dollars are involved."

The researchers documented several tactics that make these networks remarkably resilient. Organized groups hijack defunct journals — as happened with the former UK nursing publication HIV Nursing, whose lapsed domain was purchased and repurposed to publish thousands of unrelated papers indexed in Scopus. Sham peer-review processes allow papers to sail through editorial oversight. Clients can even purchase citations to burnish their apparent credibility. A companion project, accepted for publication in PLOS ONE, developed automated tools to detect paper mill output in materials science and engineering by flagging authors who misidentify the instruments used in their own experiments.

The findings carry an urgent warning about artificial intelligence. Richardson cautioned that if the scientific community cannot address existing fraud, it is unprepared for the deluge that generative AI could unleash. Fabricated papers risk contaminating the training data for future AI models, which would then produce still more questionable content — a feedback loop that could fundamentally compromise the scientific record. "We have no clue what's going to end up in the literature, what's going to be regarded as scientific fact," Richardson said.

The authors, whose work was supported by the National Science Foundation and the National Institutes of Health, called for stronger editorial monitoring, better detection tools, and structural reform of the publish-or-perish incentive systems that make fraud profitable. Amaral described the project as "probably the most depressing" of his career but framed it as an act of defense rather than an attack on science. "If you believe that science is useful and important for humanity," he said, "then you have to fight for it."

articles.headline: Murban Crude Tops $100 as Hormuz Disruption Threatens Risk Assets
articles.subheadline: The premium benchmark for oil that can bypass the Strait of Hormuz signals deep supply stress, with potential spillover into equities and bitcoin
articles.body:
Murban crude oil, the key pricing benchmark for Middle Eastern barrels that can still reliably reach global buyers without transiting the Strait of Hormuz, surged past $103 per barrel on Sunday — a level that analysts say reflects acute geopolitical stress now being priced directly into physical commodity markets.

The spike follows a week of military conflict involving the United States, Israel, and Iran, during which Iranian forces have significantly disrupted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint that normally facilitates more than $500 billion in annual oil and gas trade. The disruption has effectively split the global oil market into two tiers: barrels exposed to the strait's risks and barrels that can bypass it. Murban, a light, sweet crude produced by Abu Dhabi National Oil Company and exported through the Fujairah Oil Terminal on the UAE's eastern coast, falls squarely in the second category, making it the preferred gauge for refiners scrambling to secure prompt physical deliveries.

The $103-per-barrel price represents a significant premium over widely followed global benchmarks such as WTI and Brent, both of which have already climbed roughly 30 percent since the conflict began. When futures markets reopen Monday, traders expect those benchmarks could quickly follow Murban into triple digits, a move that would reverberate across asset classes. Higher oil prices stoke inflation expectations, potentially forcing central banks — including the Federal Reserve — to reconsider the pace of monetary easing. Markets have already begun pricing out anticipated Fed rate cuts, according to recent analyses.

For bitcoin, which traded near $67,000 on Sunday after briefly touching $74,000 earlier in the week, the implications are particularly acute. The cryptocurrency lacks underlying cash flows or revenue streams, making it acutely sensitive to shifts in fiat liquidity conditions. Tighter monetary policy driven by an energy price shock would likely pressure bitcoin alongside broader risk assets, including global equities. The leading cryptocurrency by market capitalization has already retreated roughly 9 percent from its weekly high as the geopolitical situation deteriorated.

Murban's surge above $100 underscores a structural shift in how oil is being valued during the crisis — not merely on supply-and-demand fundamentals, but on accessibility and delivery reliability. With major Asian importers including Japan, India, Thailand, and the Philippines competing for cargoes that can safely bypass the Hormuz chokepoint, the premium for secure supply routes may persist as long as the conflict continues, keeping pressure on inflation expectations and risk appetite worldwide.

articles.headline: Gulf Oil Producers Slash Output as Iran Conflict Chokes Key Waterway
articles.subheadline: Major Middle Eastern exporters forced to curtail production as storage capacity fills and critical shipping lanes remain shut
articles.body:
Oil markets face mounting turmoil as the escalating conflict in Iran forces major Gulf producers to cut output, with critical maritime shipping routes effectively closed and regional storage facilities approaching capacity, according to reporting by Bloomberg.

The disruptions, described as unprecedented in scale, center on the near-total closure of the most important waterway for global energy trade — a reference to the Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil consumption typically flows. With that chokepoint shut down, Gulf states that depend on tanker traffic through the strait have found themselves unable to move crude to market. Storage tanks are filling rapidly, leaving producers with little choice but to scale back operations.

The production curtailments mark a dramatic escalation in the economic fallout from the war in Iran, which has already roiled commodity markets in recent weeks. Markets are bracing for further volatility when trading resumes Monday, with analysts warning that the combination of constrained supply and logistical gridlock could send prices sharply higher. The situation is particularly acute for producers along the Persian Gulf coast, where export infrastructure is designed almost entirely around maritime transit through the strait.

The crisis underscores the fragility of global energy supply chains and the outsized role that a single geographic bottleneck plays in keeping oil flowing to consumers worldwide. Even producers not directly involved in the conflict are feeling the effects, as the closure disrupts shipping schedules, insurance markets, and refinery supply plans across Asia and Europe. Governments in major consuming nations are expected to face renewed pressure to tap strategic petroleum reserves or seek alternative supply arrangements.

How long the disruption persists will depend largely on the trajectory of the conflict itself and whether diplomatic efforts can restore safe passage through the strait. For now, the combination of shuttered shipping lanes, overflowing storage, and involuntary production cuts has created what market participants are calling one of the most severe supply dislocations in modern oil market history.

articles.headline: Israeli Strikes on Iranian Oil Depots Turn Tehran Skies Dark with Smoke
articles.subheadline: Residents describe explosions that 'turned night into day' as US-Israeli campaign enters second week with over 1,300 reported dead
articles.body:
Israeli airstrikes struck fuel depots in Tehran and the nearby city of Karaj overnight, sending massive fireballs into the sky and blanketing Iran's capital in thick, acrid smoke as the US-Israeli military campaign against the country entered its second week.

Residents described scenes of devastation to BBC Persian, with one saying "it was as if night had turned into day" when the strikes hit. A man in his 30s from Karaj recounted a sequence that began with "a red light that lit up everything followed by a wave that jolted the door," after which "a huge red cloud appeared." Video footage showed a Tehran street engulfed in flames near one of the targeted depots, with witnesses reporting that shops and homes had caught fire. Iranian authorities urged residents to stay indoors and said they were monitoring air quality, while foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei warned that the strikes were "releasing hazardous materials and toxic substances into the air" on a massive scale.

The strikes are part of a broader US-Israeli offensive that began with the assassination of Iran's Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. President Donald Trump has cited Iran's nuclear program as an imminent threat to the United States — a justification Tehran rejects, maintaining its nuclear activities are entirely peaceful. Iran's Assembly of Experts has reportedly reached consensus on a successor to Khamenei, though no name has been publicly announced. Iran has responded to the attacks by launching strikes against US allies and assets across the region, escalating what has become a widening Middle Eastern conflict. Iran's UN ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, said the campaign has killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands more.

Perhaps most striking are the sharply divided sentiments among ordinary Iranians. Some expressed terror at the bombardment, with one Tehran woman describing the war as "terrifying" and worrying about the aftermath. But others voiced support for the strikes as the only viable path to removing the Islamic Republic's government. "We the people have taken refuge in our homes and are eagerly awaiting the destruction of the government so we can take to the streets," a man in his 20s from Tehran told the BBC. State television has reportedly warned that anyone expressing support for the US, Israel, or Reza Pahlavi — the exiled son of Iran's last shah — "will be killed."

Another woman captured the agonizing calculus facing many Iranians: "Iranians aren't warmongers or foolish — they've just suffered so much that they've reached their limit, and all they want is a normal life." Others feared that if the regime survives the war, reprisals against the population could make conditions even worse than before. The conflict, now nine days old, shows no signs of abating, with fuel infrastructure increasingly becoming a focal point of the aerial campaign.

articles.headline: Harvard Team Uses Quantum Entanglement to Detect Starlight Photons
articles.subheadline: Experiment spanning 1.5 km fiber link could unlock a new era of ultra-high-resolution optical astronomy
articles.body:
A research team at Harvard University has demonstrated that quantum entanglement can be harnessed to detect astronomical light signals at the single-photon level, a breakthrough that could dramatically sharpen the resolving power of optical telescopes. The results, published in Nature, describe a proof-of-concept experiment led by physicist Pieter-Jan Stas that transmitted extremely faint optical signals across a fiber-optic link stretching more than 1.5 kilometers.

The work addresses one of the oldest frustrations in observational astronomy. Radio telescopes have long used a technique called very long baseline interferometry, combining signals from dishes separated by thousands of kilometers — or even placed in orbit — to achieve extraordinary angular resolution. Optical telescopes, however, have been unable to employ the same trick because the shorter wavelengths of visible light make it nearly impossible to preserve the delicate phase information needed to combine signals from widely separated collectors. Quantum entanglement, the team argues, offers a way around this fundamental barrier.

In the Harvard experiment, entangled quantum states were used to faithfully correlate photon detections at two separated nodes connected by fiber. By exploiting entanglement as a shared quantum reference, the researchers showed they could retain the phase coherence of incoming optical photons even over appreciable distances — a prerequisite for interferometric combination. The 1.5-km link is modest by astronomical standards, but it establishes the physical principles that could eventually scale to baselines of kilometers or more, yielding resolving power far beyond any single-aperture telescope in existence.

If the technique can be extended, the implications are profound.
